content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 2 Thessalonians Chapter 2 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 2:1 — "brothers" vs. "brothers and sisters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "brothers" | Literal rendering of adelphoi |
| LOGOS | "brothers" | Formal equivalent preserving the Greek masculine plural |
| PNEUMA | "brothers and sisters" | Dynamic rendering reflecting that the address includes the entire congregation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "brothers" following EMET and LOGOS. The Greek adelphoi is masculine plural; while it likely addressed a mixed congregation, the formal rendering preserves the original form. A footnote convention could address inclusive scope, but the text itself should reflect the source.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:1 — "gathering together to him" vs. "being gathered together to meet him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "our gathering-together unto him" | Literal rendering of episynagōgēs with hyphenation |
| LOGOS | "our gathering together to him" | Formal equivalent without hyphenation |
| PNEUMA | "our being gathered together to meet him" | Dynamic expansion adding "to meet" for clarity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "our gathering together to him" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "to meet him" adds a word not in the Greek. EMET's hyphenation is unnecessary in readable English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:2 — "shaken from your mind" vs. "unsettled in your thinking"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not quickly being-shaken you from the mind" | Hyper-literal participial construction |
| LOGOS | "not be quickly shaken from your mind" | Formal equivalent with natural English word order |
| PNEUMA | "not to be easily unsettled in your thinking" | Dynamic rendering; substitutes "easily" for "quickly" and "thinking" for "mind" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "not be quickly shaken from your mind" following LOGOS. The Greek tacheos means "quickly," not "easily" -- PNEUMA's substitution changes the sense. Paul's concern is the speed of their alarm, not its ease.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:2 — "be alarmed" vs. "be troubled" vs. "thrown into panic"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to-be-alarmed" | Rendering of throesthai conveying sudden disturbance |
| LOGOS | "be troubled" | Softer formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "thrown into panic" | Intensified dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "be alarmed" following EMET. The Greek throesthai (from throeō) denotes being startled or alarmed -- stronger than LOGOS's "troubled" but less extreme than PNEUMA's "panic."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET.

---

## 2:2 — "as if from us" vs. "claiming to be from us"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "as [if] through us" | Literal with bracketed supplement |
| LOGOS | "as if from us" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "claiming to be from us" | Dynamic rendering that makes the deception explicit |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "as if from us" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "claiming" adds an interpretive layer (active deception by the letter's author) that the Greek hōs di' hēmōn does not necessarily contain.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:2 — "has come" vs. "has stood upon [us]" vs. "has already come"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "has-stood-upon [us]" | Hyper-literal rendering of enestēken (perfect of enistēmi) |
| LOGOS | "has come" | Clean formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "has already come" | Adds "already" to make the perfect aspect explicit |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "has already come." The adverb "already" makes explicit what the Greek perfect tense implies -- the claim was that the Day of the Lord was a present reality. EMET's "has stood upon" is etymologically interesting but opaque in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA (with LOGOS's phrasing as base).

---

## 2:3 — "falling away" vs. "apostasy" vs. "great rebellion" for apostasia

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the falling-away" | Literal English rendering of apostasia |
| LOGOS | "the apostasy" | Formal theological term now standard in English |
| PNEUMA | "the great rebellion" | Dynamic rendering; adds "great" (not in Greek) and reinterprets "apostasy" as "rebellion" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the apostasy" following LOGOS. The term apostasia specifically denotes departure from established truth, not general rebellion. PNEUMA's addition of "great" imports a modifier absent from the Greek. "Falling away" (EMET) is accurate but less precise than "apostasy," which has become a standard theological English word.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:3 — "because [it will not come] unless" vs. "for that day will not come unless" vs. "That day will not come until"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because [it will not come] unless" | Preserves the ellipsis with bracketed supplement |
| LOGOS | "for that day will not come unless" | Supplies the ellipsis smoothly |
| PNEUMA | "That day will not come until" | New sentence; uses "until" instead of "unless" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "for that day will not come unless" following LOGOS. The Greek hoti ean mē is best rendered "for... unless" rather than "until," which subtly changes the conditional logic. EMET's brackets expose the ellipsis but hinder readability.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:3 — "is revealed" vs. "is revealed -- the one who is destined for destruction"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the son of-destruction" | Literal Semitic idiom |
| LOGOS | "the son of destruction" | Formal equivalent preserving the idiom |
| PNEUMA | "the one who is destined for destruction" | Dynamic rendering unpacking the Semitic idiom |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the son of destruction" following EMET/LOGOS. The Semitic idiom "son of X" (denoting one characterized by or destined for X) is well-known in biblical English and should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:4 — "every [thing] being called God" vs. "every so-called god" vs. "every so-called god"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "every [thing] being-called God" | Literal rendering of legomenon theon with capitalized "God" |
| LOGOS | "every so-called god" | Formal rendering with lowercase "god" to indicate false deities |
| PNEUMA | "every so-called god" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "every so-called god" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. The lowercase "god" correctly indicates that the participle legomenon qualifies the noun -- these are entities bearing the title without the reality.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:4 — "sits down" vs. "sits" vs. "seats himself"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sits-down" | Literal rendering of kathisai |
| LOGOS | "he sits" | Simple formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "he seats himself" | Reflexive rendering emphasizing deliberate action |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he seats himself" following PNEUMA. The middle voice force of kathisai (aorist infinitive) and the context of self-exaltation support the reflexive rendering -- this is a deliberate act of enthronement, not passive sitting.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA.

---

## 2:4 — "showing forth himself that he is God" vs. "declaring himself to be God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "showing-forth himself that he is God" | Literal rendering of apodeiknunta |
| LOGOS | "declaring himself to be God" | Formal rendering conveying the public demonstration |
| PNEUMA | "declaring himself to be God" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "declaring himself to be God" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Greek apodeiknumi means "to show forth, to demonstrate, to proclaim publicly." While "showing forth" is etymologically closer, "declaring" captures the performative, public nature of the claim in natural English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:5 — "I was saying these things to you" vs. "I was telling you these things"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I-was-saying these-things to-you" | Literal rendering with imperfect tense (elegon) |
| LOGOS | "I was telling you these things" | Formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "I told you these things" | Simple past instead of imperfect |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "I was telling you these things" following LOGOS. The Greek imperfect elegon conveys repeated or ongoing action -- "I was telling" (iterative imperfect) is more accurate than PNEUMA's simple past "I told," which suggests a single occasion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:6 — "the thing restraining you know" vs. "you know that which restrains" vs. "you know what is currently holding him back"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-[thing]-restraining you-know" | Literal word order preserving the neuter participle to katechon |
| LOGOS | "you know that which restrains" | Formal rendering with natural English word order |
| PNEUMA | "you know what is currently holding him back" | Dynamic expansion adding "currently" and specifying "him" as the object |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "you know that which restrains" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's additions ("currently," "him") import specificity not present in the Greek. The deliberate ambiguity of to katechon must be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:6 — "unto the being revealed him in his own time" vs. "for him to be revealed in his own time" vs. "so that he will be revealed only when his time comes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unto the being-revealed him in-his-own time" | Hyper-literal articular infinitive |
| LOGOS | "for him to be revealed in his own time" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "so that he will be revealed only when his time comes" | Dynamic expansion adding "only" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "so that he will be revealed in his own time" -- a blend. The eis to + infinitive construction expresses purpose/result, which "so that" captures better than LOGOS's "for." However, PNEUMA's added "only" is not in the Greek and is omitted.

**Status:** Resolved as SOPHIA blend.

---

## 2:7 — "mystery of lawlessness" vs. "hidden power of lawlessness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the mystery of-lawlessness" | Literal rendering of to mystērion tēs anomias |
| LOGOS | "the mystery of lawlessness" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the hidden power of lawlessness" | Dynamic rendering interpreting mystērion as "hidden power" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the mystery of lawlessness" following EMET/LOGOS. The word mystērion in Pauline usage refers to something previously hidden but now revealed by God, or something whose full reality is not yet manifest. PNEUMA's "hidden power" is interpretive -- it reduces "mystery" to mere concealment and adds "power" without textual warrant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:7 — "until out of the midst he comes" vs. "until he is out of the way" vs. "until he is removed from the way"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "until out-of [the] midst he-comes" | Literal rendering of heōs ek mesou genētai |
| LOGOS | "until he is out of the way" | Formal idiom |
| PNEUMA | "until he is removed from the way" | Dynamic rendering implying passive removal |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "until he is out of the way" following LOGOS. The Greek genētai (aorist subjunctive of ginomai) is neutral -- "comes to be / becomes." PNEUMA's "removed" implies an external agent doing the removing, which is an interpretive addition. EMET's "out of the midst he comes" is formally accurate but awkward.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:8 — "will slay" vs. "will destroy" for anelei

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "will-slay" | Literal rendering of anelei (from anaireō, "to take up, to slay") |
| LOGOS | "will slay" | Formal equivalent following Sinaiticus |
| PNEUMA | "will destroy" | More general term |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "will slay" following EMET/LOGOS. Sinaiticus reads anelei, not analōsei. The verb anaireō means specifically "to kill, to slay" -- a more pointed term than the generic "destroy." The specificity is deliberate: Christ kills the lawless one, not merely defeats him.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:8 — "spirit of his mouth" vs. "breath of his mouth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the spirit of-his mouth" | Literal rendering of tō pneumati tou stomatos; preserves the double meaning of pneuma (spirit/breath) |
| LOGOS | "the breath of his mouth" | Contextual rendering following the Isaiah 11:4 allusion |
| PNEUMA | "the breath of his mouth" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the breath of his mouth" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. While pneuma can mean "spirit," the phrase "pneuma of his mouth" in context (echoing Isaiah 11:4 LXX) clearly means "breath." The image is of Christ's word/breath as a lethal force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:8 — "will bring to nothing" vs. "bring to nothing" vs. "wipe out"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "will-bring-to-nothing" | Literal rendering of katargēsei |
| LOGOS | "bring to nothing" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "wipe out" | Dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "bring to nothing" following EMET/LOGOS. The Greek katargeō means "to render ineffective, to nullify, to abolish." "Bring to nothing" preserves the devastating understatement: the lawless one is not just killed but reduced to nothingness.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:8 — "manifestation of his coming" vs. "sheer brilliance of his coming"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the manifestation of-his coming" | Literal rendering of tē epiphaneia tēs parousias |
| LOGOS | "the manifestation of his coming" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the sheer brilliance of his coming" | Dynamic rendering interpreting epiphaneia as visible radiance |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the manifestation of his coming" following EMET/LOGOS. The Greek epiphaneia means "appearing, manifestation" -- it is a technical eschatological term. PNEUMA's "sheer brilliance" is evocative but interpretive; epiphaneia emphasizes visibility and public appearance, not necessarily luminosity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:9 — "wonders of falsehood" vs. "lying wonders" vs. "counterfeit miracles, signs, and wonders"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "signs and wonders of-falsehood" | Literal genitive; "of falsehood" modifies both signs and wonders |
| LOGOS | "signs and lying wonders" | Formal rendering with attributive adjective |
| PNEUMA | "counterfeit miracles, signs, and wonders" | Dynamic expansion; adds "miracles" and restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "signs and lying wonders" following LOGOS. The Greek pseudous ("of falsehood") is a genitive modifier on the whole phrase. PNEUMA's addition of "counterfeit miracles" introduces a word (miracles) not in the Greek and restructures the triad "power and signs and wonders" into a different grouping. LOGOS's "lying" captures the deceptive quality concisely.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:9 — "in all power" vs. "with all power" vs. "all kinds of"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in all power" | Literal rendering of en pasē dynamei |
| LOGOS | "with all power" | Formal rendering with instrumental "with" |
| PNEUMA | "in all kinds of" | Dynamic expansion interpreting pasē as "all kinds of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with all power" following LOGOS. The preposition en here functions instrumentally ("by means of"), which "with" captures naturally. PNEUMA's "all kinds of" is a looser paraphrase.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:10 — "every deceit of unrighteousness" vs. "every deception of unrighteousness" vs. "every sort of evil deception"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "every deceit of-unrighteousness" | Literal genitive |
| LOGOS | "every deception of unrighteousness" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "every sort of evil deception" | Dynamic rendering; replaces "unrighteousness" with "evil" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "every deception of unrighteousness" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's substitution of "evil" for "unrighteousness" (adikias) loses the specific legal/covenantal connotation of adikia -- it is not generic evil but the opposite of righteousness (dikaiosynē).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:10 — "in the ones perishing" vs. "for those who are perishing" vs. "aimed at those who are perishing"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the-[ones]-perishing" | Literal dative/locative |
| LOGOS | "for those who are perishing" | Formal rendering with "for" |
| PNEUMA | "aimed at those who are perishing" | Dynamic rendering adding intentionality |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "among those who are perishing" -- a SOPHIA blend. The Greek tois apollymenois is a dative participle. "Among" captures the locative sense (the deception operates within the sphere of those perishing) better than LOGOS's "for" (which could imply purpose) or PNEUMA's "aimed at" (which adds agency).

**Status:** Resolved as SOPHIA blend.

---

## 2:10 — "in return for which... they did not receive" vs. "because they did not receive" vs. "because they refused to love"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in-return-for-which the love of-the truth they-did-not-receive" | Literal rendering of anth' hōn with causal force |
| LOGOS | "because they did not receive the love of the truth" | Formal causal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "because they refused to love the truth" | Dynamic rendering; changes "did not receive the love of the truth" to "refused to love the truth" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "because they did not receive the love of the truth" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "refused to love the truth" changes the structure -- the Greek says they failed to receive "the love of the truth" (tēn agapēn tēs alētheias), where "love" is the object received, not an action they refused to perform. The nuance matters: they were offered a love-for-truth and declined it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:10 — "unto the being saved them" vs. "so that they might be saved" vs. "and so be saved"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unto the being-saved them" | Literal articular infinitive |
| LOGOS | "so that they might be saved" | Formal purpose clause |
| PNEUMA | "and so be saved" | Simplified dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "so that they might be saved" following LOGOS. The eis to + infinitive construction expresses purpose: they failed to receive the love of the truth for the purpose of being saved. PNEUMA's "and so" weakens the purposive force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:11 — "a working of delusion" vs. "powerful delusion"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a-working of-delusion" | Literal rendering of energeian planēs |
| LOGOS | "a working of delusion" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "a powerful delusion" | Dynamic rendering interpreting energeian as "powerful" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a working of delusion" following EMET/LOGOS. The Greek energeian means "working, operation, active force" -- not merely "powerful." PNEUMA's rendering loses the crucial sense that God sends an active operative agent of delusion, not just a strong feeling of confusion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:11 — "so that they might believe" vs. "so that they will believe"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unto the believing them the falsehood" | Literal articular infinitive |
| LOGOS | "so that they might believe the lie" | Subjunctive rendering |
| PNEUMA | "so that they will believe the lie" | Future indicative rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "so that they will believe the lie" following PNEUMA. The eis to + infinitive here expresses intended result -- the delusion is sent with the expected outcome that they will believe. The indicative "will" captures the certainty of the divine judicial action better than the subjunctive "might."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA.

---

## 2:11 — "the falsehood" vs. "the lie"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the falsehood" | Literal rendering of to pseudos |
| LOGOS | "the lie" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the lie" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the lie" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. The definite article (to pseudos, "the lie/falsehood") points to a specific lie -- the deception of the lawless one. "The lie" is more pointed and concrete in English than "the falsehood."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:12 — "might be judged" vs. "may be judged" vs. "will be condemned"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "might-be-judged" | Aorist subjunctive rendering of krithōsin |
| LOGOS | "may be judged" | Formal subjunctive |
| PNEUMA | "will be condemned" | Dynamic rendering; uses "condemned" (reflecting the variant katakrithōsin) and future indicative |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "may be judged" following LOGOS. Sinaiticus reads krithōsin (from krinō, "to judge"), not katakrithōsin ("to condemn"). PNEUMA follows the variant reading, which is not the Sinaiticus text. The subjunctive "may" preserves the modal force of the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:12 — "having delighted in" vs. "took pleasure in" vs. "have taken pleasure in"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having-delighted in unrighteousness" | Literal aorist participle |
| LOGOS | "took pleasure in unrighteousness" | Formal past tense |
| PNEUMA | "have taken pleasure in wickedness" | Dynamic rendering; substitutes "wickedness" for "unrighteousness" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "took pleasure in unrighteousness" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "wickedness" (for adikia) loses the specific antithesis with "truth" (alētheia) and "righteousness" (dikaiosynē) that structures the entire passage. The contrast is truth/righteousness vs. lie/unrighteousness.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:13 — "from the beginning" vs. "as firstfruits" for ap' archēs / aparchēn

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from [the] beginning" | Follows Sinaiticus reading ap' archēs |
| LOGOS | "from the beginning" | Same Sinaiticus reading |
| PNEUMA | "as firstfruits" | Follows Vaticanus (B) reading aparchēn, arguing it is more likely original |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "from the beginning" following EMET/LOGOS and the Sinaiticus text. This is the most significant textual variant in the chapter. The difference in uncial script is a single letter (ΑΠ ΑΡΧΗC vs. ΑΠΑΡΧΗΝ). Both readings are ancient and well-attested. As this project follows Codex Sinaiticus as its source manuscript, "from the beginning" is adopted. The variant "as firstfruits" (supported by B and other witnesses) is noted in the Council Notes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS (Sinaiticus reading). PNEUMA's variant noted.

---

## 2:13 — "brothers having been loved by the Lord" vs. "brothers beloved by the Lord" vs. "brothers and sisters loved by the Lord"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "brothers having-been-loved by [the] Lord" | Literal perfect passive participle |
| LOGOS | "brothers beloved by the Lord" | Formal rendering with adjectival "beloved" |
| PNEUMA | "brothers and sisters loved by the Lord" | Dynamic rendering with gender-inclusive address |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "brothers beloved by the Lord" following LOGOS. "Beloved" as an adjective is more natural than EMET's participial construction, and "brothers" follows Sinaiticus (see v.1 resolution). The perfect participle ēgapēmenoi conveys an enduring state, which "beloved" captures well.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:13 — "in sanctification of the Spirit and faith of the truth" vs. "through sanctification by the Spirit and belief in the truth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in sanctification of-[the]-Spirit and faith of-[the]-truth" | Literal genitives preserved |
| LOGOS | "through sanctification by the Spirit and belief in the truth" | Formal rendering interpreting the genitives |
| PNEUMA | "through the sanctifying work of the Spirit and through belief in the truth" | Dynamic expansion; adds "work" and repeats "through" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "through sanctification by the Spirit and belief in the truth" following LOGOS. The preposition en + dative here is instrumental ("through/by means of"). LOGOS correctly interprets the ambiguous genitives: "sanctification by the Spirit" (subjective genitive -- the Spirit sanctifies) and "belief in the truth" (objective genitive -- truth is what is believed). PNEUMA's addition of "work" is unnecessary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:14 — "good news" vs. "gospel" vs. "proclamation of the good news"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "our good-news" | Literal rendering of euangelion |
| LOGOS | "our gospel" | Standard formal term |
| PNEUMA | "our proclamation of the good news" | Dynamic expansion adding "proclamation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "our gospel" following LOGOS. While EMET's "good news" is etymologically precise, "gospel" is the established English rendering of euangelion in theological discourse. PNEUMA adds "proclamation" (not in the Greek), which shifts the emphasis from the content to the act of delivering it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:14 — "obtaining of the glory" vs. "share in the glory"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unto [the] obtaining of-[the]-glory" | Literal rendering of eis peripoiēsin doxēs |
| LOGOS | "for the obtaining of the glory" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "so that you might share in the glory" | Dynamic rendering interpreting peripoiēsis as "sharing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "for the obtaining of the glory" following LOGOS. The Greek peripoiēsis means "obtaining, acquisition, possession" -- it is active appropriation, not passive sharing. PNEUMA softens the force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:15 — "hold fast" vs. "hold to" vs. "hold fast to"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "hold-fast" | Literal rendering of krateite |
| LOGOS | "hold to" | Simpler formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "hold fast to" | Combines EMET's intensity with LOGOS's preposition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "hold fast to" following PNEUMA. The verb krateō means "to seize, to hold firmly, to grasp tightly." "Hold fast to" captures the urgency better than LOGOS's simple "hold to."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA.

---

## 2:15 — "whether through word whether through letter of us" vs. "whether by word or by our letter" vs. "whether by what we said in person or by letter"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "whether through word whether through letter of-us" | Literal double "whether" construction |
| LOGOS | "whether by word or by our letter" | Formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "whether by what we said in person or by letter" | Dynamic expansion adding "in person" and "what we said" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "whether by word or by our letter" following LOGOS. PNEUMA's "in person" and "what we said" are interpretive additions not in the Greek. The simple "word" (logos) stands for oral instruction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:16 — "the one having loved us" vs. "who loved us"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-[one]-having-loved us" | Literal aorist participle |
| LOGOS | "who loved us" | Formal relative clause |
| PNEUMA | "who loved us" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "who loved us" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. The participial-to-relative-clause conversion is standard in English translation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:16 — "eternal encouragement" vs. "eternal comfort"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "eternal encouragement" | Rendering paraklēsin aiōnian with the broader sense of paraklēsis |
| LOGOS | "eternal comfort" | Formal rendering emphasizing the consolation aspect |
| PNEUMA | "eternal comfort" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "eternal comfort" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. While paraklēsis encompasses encouragement, exhortation, and comfort, the context here (a prayer for hearts troubled by eschatological anxiety) favors "comfort." The same root is used in v.17 (parakalasai), creating a wordplay preserved in the consensus rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:16 — "God the Father of us" vs. "God our Father"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "God the Father of-us" | Literal word order following Sinaiticus |
| LOGOS | "God our Father" | Natural English word order |
| PNEUMA | "God our Father" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "God our Father" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. The possessive "our" naturally precedes "Father" in English without loss of meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:17 — "encourage" vs. "comfort" vs. "encourage"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "encourage" | Rendering parakalasai as encouragement |
| LOGOS | "comfort" | Rendering parakalasai as comfort (matching v.16) |
| PNEUMA | "encourage" | Rendering parakalasai as encouragement |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "comfort" following LOGOS, to maintain consistency with "eternal comfort" in v.16 and preserve the wordplay (paraklēsis/parakalasai). The God who gave eternal comfort is asked to comfort their hearts.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:17 — "establish [you]" vs. "establish them" vs. "strengthen you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "establish [you]" | Literal rendering of stērixai with bracketed object |
| LOGOS | "establish them" | Uses "them" referring to "hearts" |
| PNEUMA | "strengthen you" | Dynamic rendering with different verb and pronoun |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "establish you." The verb stērixai means "to fix firmly, to establish, to make stable" -- "establish" is more precise than PNEUMA's "strengthen." The object is ultimately the Thessalonians themselves (not just their hearts), so "you" is adopted over LOGOS's "them."

**Status:** Resolved as SOPHIA blend (EMET's verb + direct address).

---

## 2:17 — "work and word" order

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "work and word" | Follows Sinaiticus order (ergō kai logō) |
| LOGOS | "work and word" | Same Sinaiticus order |
| PNEUMA | "work and word" | Same Sinaiticus order |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three translators agree on the Sinaiticus word order "work and word," placing practice before speech. No disagreement.

**Status:** Unanimous. Included for completeness as some manuscripts reverse the order.

---

**Summary:** Of 17 verses, all 17 contained at least one point of divergence among the translators. The most significant disagreements concerned: (1) "apostasy" vs. "falling away" vs. "great rebellion" in v.3; (2) "from the beginning" vs. "as firstfruits" in v.13 (a textual variant); (3) "working of delusion" vs. "powerful delusion" in v.11; and (4) "judged" vs. "condemned" in v.12 (another textual variant). LOGOS's renderings were adopted most frequently, reflecting its balance of accuracy and readability. EMET's contributions were essential for identifying the precise Greek forms. PNEUMA's dynamic renderings, while communicatively effective, frequently added words or interpretive layers not present in the source text.

— SOPHIA, Arbiter
